reasoning: Recent reports from March 11, 2026 (Phys.org) indicate that the Arctic sea ice reached an apparent early maximum of nearly 14.22 million square kilometers on March 10, and has subsequently begun to decline. This is significantly lower than the 2025 record year, which saw a maximum of 14.33 million sq km on March 22. With the peak being roughly 0.11 million sq km lower and the decline phase starting nearly two weeks earlier, the March 2026 average daily extent is mathematically primed to finish below the March 2025 average of 14.1 million sq km. However, late-March weather fluctuations can occasionally cause secondary growth spikes. A "could break last year's record low" headline indicates high possibility rather than absolute certainty. Given the extremely favorable trajectory versus the inherent uncertainty of predicting the second half of March, a coin-flip or slightly higher probability is well justified, updating significantly from the prior crowd estimate of 0.20 on March 6.
